title: Aging Iranian VLCC Nasha resurfaces at Kharg Island
summary: TankerTrackers and UANI satellite imagery shows the 1997-built Iran-flagged VLCC Nasha docked at Kharg Island on April 26 after years off public tracking systems, suggesting Tehran reactivates older hulls amid blockade-driven storage strain.
contents:
# Aging Iranian VLCC Nasha returns to Kharg Island

## Overview
TankerTrackers and the advocacy group United Against Nuclear Iran (UANI) reported satellite imagery showing the Nasha, a 1997-built Iran-flagged Very Large Crude Carrier (VLCC), docked at Kharg Island on April 26, 2026. The decades-old supertanker had remained absent from public tracking systems for years before this reappearance.

## Vessel Details
- **Name**: Nasha
- **Type**: VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier)
- **Built**: 1997
- **Flag**: Iran

## Significance
Analysts interpret the reactivation of older Iranian-flagged hulls as evidence that Tehran faces mounting strain on crude storage capacity under the US naval blockade. Bringing previously retired vessels back into rotation suggests Iran needs additional floating storage and loading hulls to absorb surplus production. Kharg Island handles the majority of Iran's crude exports, and continued loading activity there indicates Tehran retains operational capability at its primary export hub despite expanded enforcement pressure since the April 13 blockade declaration.

## Source Context
Bloomberg News reviewed the satellite imagery alongside UANI analysis. The find adds to a pattern of Iran reactivating dormant tonnage and pushing tanker activity outside conventional sanctioned-fleet inventories.

title: Iranian oil tankers cluster off Chabahar near US blockade line
summary: TankerTrackers, Windward, and UANI satellite imagery shows six to eight Iran-laden VLCCs idling near Chabahar in the Gulf of Oman just inside the US blockade boundary, with smaller tankers gathered nearby.
contents:
# Iranian Tankers Cluster Off Chabahar Port

## Overview
TankerTrackers and other maritime intelligence firms reported approximately six to eight Iranian-laden supertankers idling near Chabahar, an Iranian port outside the Persian Gulf and just inside the US blockade boundary. Smaller tankers also gathered nearby. The clustering activity shows tankers seeking to position cargoes for movement past US enforcement.

## Location
- **Port**: Chabahar, Iran
- **Region**: Gulf of Oman
- **Approximate Coordinates**: 25.29N, 60.64E

## Activity Pattern
Satellite imagery reviewed by United Against Nuclear Iran (UANI), Windward, and TankerTrackers showed 6-8 VLCCs idling near Chabahar along with a smaller fleet of Aframax and Suezmax tankers. The same area earlier hosted two very large crude carriers redirected by US Navy interceptors. Some vessels turned off transponders before approaching the blockade line.

## Significance
The Chabahar staging zone provides Iran with a southeastern alternative to the Strait of Hormuz, allowing tankers to load at Iranian export terminals and then linger outside Persian Gulf chokepoints. The pattern suggests Tehran arranges floating storage close to the blockade boundary, ready to push cargoes through dark transits or to test US enforcement response. Independent satellite analysis from TankerTrackers earlier counted nine Iranian tankers gathered at Chabahar, up from five just two days earlier, signaling mounting congestion as enforcement pressure builds.

## Tags
tanker-tracking, iran, chabahar, gulf-of-oman, blockade, dark-fleet

title: TankerTrackers: Iran loaded 4.6M barrels, 4M crossed US blockade in covert Exfiltration
summary: TankerTrackers reported on April 27 that Iran loaded approximately 4.6 million barrels of crude at export terminals while another 4 million barrels appeared to have crossed the US blockade line in what the firm labeled an Exfiltration operation.
contents:
# Iranian Crude Loading and Exfiltration Continue Despite Blockade

## Headline Findings
On April 27, 2026, TankerTrackers published satellite-derived figures showing roughly 4.6 million barrels of crude loaded at Iranian export terminals, while approximately 4 million barrels appeared to have crossed the US blockade line. TankerTrackers labeled the covert outbound movement an "Exfiltration" operation.

## Loading Activity
- **Loaded at Iranian terminals**: ~4.6 million barrels
- **Crossed blockade line**: ~4 million barrels (covert routing)

## Methodology
TankerTrackers analyzed daily satellite imagery, AIS feeds, and shoreside photography to detect both loading at Kharg Island and clandestine outbound movement past US naval positions. Some tankers transmitted AIS while moving along the Omani coastline as a public test of US deterrence; others switched off transponders and proceeded under cover.

## Geopolitical Context
Washington imposed a maritime blockade on Iranian ports on April 13, 2026, after talks collapsed. The blockade targets roughly 2 million barrels per day of Iranian oil exports, most of which normally flow toward Chinese buyers. TankerTrackers data suggests Tehran's export network adapts to enforcement pressure by combining floating storage in the Gulf of Oman, ship-to-ship transfers near the Riau Archipelago, and dark transits past US interdiction lines.

## Tags
tanker-tracking, iran, sanctions-evasion, dark-fleet, blockade, exfiltration, kharg-island

title: TankerTrackers tracks two laden VLCCs past US blockade with ~4M barrels Iranian crude
summary: TankerTrackers data dated April 24 shows two VLCCs carrying roughly 4 million barrels of Iranian crude moved past the US-declared blockade line bound for Asian markets, transiting under selective AIS visibility.
contents:
# Two VLCCs Past US Blockade Carrying ~4 Million Barrels Iranian Crude

## Reported Activity
TankerTrackers data dated April 24, 2026 shows two very large crude carriers (VLCCs) carrying approximately 4 million barrels of Iranian crude oil moved past the US-declared blockade line and proceeded toward Asian markets. The vessels operated under selective AIS visibility, switching transponders off during the most sensitive transit segments.

## Movement Profile
- **Vessels**: 2 VLCCs (collective ~4 million barrels)
- **Route**: Persian Gulf → Gulf of Oman → Arabian Sea → Asian destinations
- **AIS status**: Intermittent / dark during blockade transit

## Wider Picture
Vortexa and TankerTrackers separately tracked at least 34 Iran-linked tankers and gas carriers passing through the Strait of Hormuz and the US blockade line since the embargo took effect on April 13. Of those, 19 headed outbound, with 17 carrying cargo. Four empty Iranian tankers returning from Asia turned up off Pakistan's coast. US forces redirected dozens of vessels and boarded the sanctioned tanker Tifani in Southeast Asia, yet a steady flow of dark-fleet movement continues to feed Chinese buyers.

## Implications
The reported transits show that the blockade slows but does not entirely halt Iranian crude flowing east. Floating storage west of Hormuz, ship-to-ship transfers in the Riau Archipelago, and AIS spoofing all support sustained exports despite tightened US enforcement.

## Tags
tanker-tracking, iran, vlcc, blockade, dark-fleet, sanctions, china, asia

title: TankerTrackers tracks US boarding of sanctioned tanker Majestic X in Indian Ocean
summary: TankerTrackers reported AIS data showing US forces conducted a right-of-visit boarding of the sanctioned stateless tanker Majestic X (formerly Phonix, Eternity) at 0201 UTC on April 24 in the Indian Ocean near 5.72N 86.94E.
contents:
# US Forces Board Sanctioned Tanker Majestic X in Indian Ocean

## Event
TankerTrackers tracked AIS data indicating US forces conducted a maritime interdiction and "right-of-visit" boarding of the sanctioned stateless tanker Majestic X in the Indian Ocean at approximately 0201 UTC on April 24, 2026, near coordinates 5.72N, 86.94E.

## Vessel
- **Current Name**: Majestic X
- **Aliases**: Eternity (former), Phonix (former)
- **Flag**: Stateless (previously Guyana, falsely registered)
- **OFAC Status**: Sanctioned December 2024 (Executive Order 13902)
- **Linked Manager**: Vision Ship Management LLP

## Cargo and History
According to TankerTrackers, the vessel transported roughly 20 million barrels of Iranian crude while operating under a false Guyanese flag without authorization. The boarding marked the first known direct US interdiction involving the vessel.

## Operational Context
The interdiction occurred in the US Indo-Pacific Command area of responsibility, well east of the Strait of Hormuz, signaling that Washington extends maritime enforcement against Iranian oil shipments far beyond the Gulf chokepoint. The operation followed a string of US enforcement actions, including the April 21 boarding of the tanker Tifani in Southeast Asia and CENTCOM's redirection of more than two dozen vessels under the blockade.

## Significance
The Majestic X operation underlines a global pivot in US sanctions enforcement, expanding from Hormuz-area chokepoints to deep-water interdiction across the Arabian Sea and Indian Ocean. Owners and insurers of dark-fleet tonnage now face direct military boarding risk in waters formerly considered low-enforcement zones.

## Tags

title: National Iranian VLCC returns to Kharg Island after Riau STS transfer
summary: TankerTrackers tweeted on April 21 that a National Iranian VLCC supertanker departed Iran in late March 2026, transferred 2 million barrels of crude to another VLCC near the Riau Archipelago, then crossed the US blockade line on its return to Kharg Island.
contents:
# National Iranian VLCC Returns to Kharg Island via Blockade Line

## Tweet Summary
TankerTrackers posted on April 21, 2026: a National Iranian VLCC supertanker departed Iran in late March 2026 and sailed to the Riau Archipelago, where the crew transferred 2 million barrels of crude oil to another VLCC via ship-to-ship operation. The same VLCC then crossed the US blockade line on its return voyage and reached Kharg Island the following day.

## Voyage Profile
- **Departed Iran**: late March 2026
- **STS Location**: Riau Archipelago (Indonesia)
- **Cargo Transferred**: 2 million barrels of Iranian crude
- **Return Path**: across US-declared blockade line back into the Persian Gulf
- **Final Port**: Kharg Island, Iran

## Operational Context
Riau Archipelago waters function as a long-standing transfer zone, where Iran-linked tankers offload crude to other vessels for onward delivery to Chinese refiners. Vessels switch off AIS during transfers and reappear only when entering or leaving the Strait of Malacca. The ship's safe return through the blockade line shows that some VLCCs continue to evade US Navy enforcement, despite Washington's claim of an airtight cordon.

## Wider Picture
Earlier the same week, US Marines boarded the sanctioned VLCC Tifani in the Indian Ocean. CENTCOM stated US forces directed 28 vessels to turn around or return to port since the blockade started on April 13. Iran-linked tonnage continues to test enforcement gaps along the Iranian coast and through the southern Hormuz corridor.

contents:
# 9 million barrels exit Gulf of Oman floating storage since US blockade — TankerTrackers

## Overview
Vessel-monitoring service TankerTrackers reports that Iran shipped approximately 9 million barrels of crude oil from floating storage in the Gulf of Oman since the US naval blockade took effect on April 13, 2026 at 1400 GMT. An additional 2 million barrels departed on April 12, the day before enforcement started, according to tracker data published via Anadolu Agency.

## Volume tracked
- 9 million barrels: shipped from floating storage after the blockade began
- 2 million barrels: departed April 12, ahead of the deadline
- 11 vessels: passed through the Strait of Hormuz in the 24 hours leading up to 0900 GMT Thursday
- ~2 million barrels per day: typical Iranian export flow targeted by the blockade, most of it normally heading to China

## Strategic context
Washington announced the blockade following the collapse of talks with Tehran. The operation aims to prevent ships from traveling to and from Iranian ports. Multiple vessels reversed course since the start of enforcement, and shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz thinned sharply. Traders and shipowners track whether Tehran can keep exports flowing through alternative routes, floating storage, or shadow-fleet transfers.

## Loophole testing
Some sanctioned or Iran-linked tankers continued attempts to navigate the region. Two US-sanctioned supertankers entered the Gulf this week despite the blockade. Other vessels turned back after approaching or crossing Hormuz, illustrating an uneven enforcement picture.

## Significance
The 9 million barrel exit indicates Tehran moved quickly to clear pre-positioned floating storage before tighter restrictions took hold. The pattern suggests an effort to monetize stranded inventory ahead of harsher conditions while testing the operational limits of US naval interdiction.

## Tags
tanker-tracking, iran, gulf-of-oman, floating-storage, sanctions, blockade, shadow-fleet, kharg-island, strait-of-hormuz

title: TankerTrackers: 236 Iran-linked tankers escape US sanctions, 72 active in Middle East
summary: Maritime tracker TankerTrackers identifies 633 active Iran-linked tankers tracked over the years, of which 397 face OFAC sanctions and 236 remain outside US sanctions. The platform reports 72 unsanctioned vessels currently moving freely in the Middle East, while satellite imagery from April 16 shows 3 tankers loading 5 million barrels at Kharg Island.
contents:
# 236 Iran-linked tankers remain outside US sanctions — TankerTrackers

## Overview
Maritime tracker platform TankerTrackers identified 633 active Iran-linked tankers tracked over the years, with 397 designated by the US Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC). That leaves 236 vessels outside US sanctions, including 72 currently moving freely across the Middle East, according to data published via Anadolu Agency on April 17, 2026.

## Fleet breakdown
- 633 total: active Iran-linked tankers tracked over the years
- 397 vessels: under OFAC sanctions
- 236 vessels: outside US sanctions
- 72 vessels: unsanctioned hulls currently moving in the Middle East

## Spoofing tactics
Some vessels broadcast false Automatic Identification System (AIS) signals to mask their identities. Such spoofing tactics, alongside ships going dark or shifting cargoes between vessels, present a central obstacle for authorities trying to identify and stop sanctioned oil movements. Cargo transfers between hulls help obscure origin documentation that downstream buyers rely on for plausible deniability.

## Kharg Island still loading
A separate satellite imagery assessment shows three tankers loading approximately 5 million barrels at Iran's Kharg Island export hub on April 16, 2026, despite mounting pressure on Tehran's oil trade. Continued loading at Kharg Island suggests Tehran still places crude onto tankers despite tighter surveillance and growing pressure on its maritime export network.

## Implications
The TankerTrackers numbers expose a structural gap: a substantial share of the Iran-linked fleet operates outside the formal US sanctions perimeter. Unsanctioned hulls give Tehran operational flexibility, particularly for ship-to-ship transfers and route obfuscation. Washington faces an enforcement gap unless additional designations cover the remaining 236 vessels.

title: TankerTrackers: 2 million barrels of Iranian crude reach India under US sanctions waiver
summary: TankerTrackers data shows 2 million barrels of Iranian crude oil reached India since March 20 under US Treasury OFAC General License U, which allows pre-March 20 cargoes to discharge through April 19. India confirmed resumption of Iranian oil purchases for the first time in seven years amid the Iran war.
contents:
# 2 million barrels of Iranian crude reach India under US sanctions waiver — TankerTrackers

## Overview
Two million barrels of Iranian crude oil reached India under a US sanctions waiver since March 20, according to TankerTrackers data published April 10, 2026. The shipment marks a rare delivery of Iranian oil to India under a temporary US Treasury Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) authorization that allows previously loaded cargoes to discharge through April 19.

## License details
On March 20, OFAC issued Iran General License U, authorizing transactions ordinarily necessary for the sale, delivery, and offloading of Iranian-origin crude oil and petroleum products loaded on vessels on or before March 20. The license permits docking, anchoring, tug assistance, bunkering, inspections, pilotage, and discharge of covered cargoes. The window provides a temporary path for previously loaded Iranian shipments to complete delivery.

## India resumes Iranian purchases
India announced resumption of oil purchases from Iran for the first time since 2019, when Washington declined to renew waivers on Iranian oil. The announcement marks the first public confirmation of an Iranian crude delivery to India in seven years. The development could open a limited resumption of Iranian crude arrivals to major Asian buyers as war-related disruptions reshape regional energy flows.

## Strait of Hormuz disruption
Shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical global oil chokepoint, has faced disruption since the US and Israel launched an air offensive against Iran on February 28. India's Petroleum Minister Hardeep Singh Puri met with Qatari Minister of State for Energy Affairs Saad Sherida Al-Kaabi in Doha, where the two stressed the importance of restoring normal global energy supplies and emphasized unimpeded freedom of navigation.

## Energy supply implications
The delivery underscores how war-related disruptions and shipping risks around the Strait of Hormuz reshape regional energy flows. India imported 41.4% of its LNG from Qatar in fiscal year 2024-25, leaving New Delhi exposed to Persian Gulf supply shocks. Reopening an Iranian channel — even via a narrow waiver — diversifies supply for Indian refiners during the conflict.

## Tags
tanker-tracking, iran, india, ofac, sanctions-waiver, crude-oil, strait-of-hormuz, qatar, lng, energy

title: TankerTrackers: Sanctioned tanker breaches US blockade with AIS broadcasting along Oman coast
summary: TankerTrackers reports a US-sanctioned supertanker breached the US naval blockade while keeping its AIS transponder operational along the Omani coast. The platform also tracks up to 20 empty supertankers available for loading or floating storage, four of which remain outside US sanctions, plus a VLCC carrying 2 million barrels still anchored inside Iranian waters.
contents:
# Sanctioned tanker breaches US blockade with AIS on along Oman coast — TankerTrackers

## Overview
TankerTrackers reports that a US-sanctioned supertanker breached the maritime blockade declared by Washington while keeping its Automatic Identification System (AIS) operational along the coast of Oman, according to a Voice of Emirates summary published April 28, 2026. Analysts interpret the move as a deliberate public test of US maritime deterrence and Washington's capacity to enforce the blockade.

## Key data points
- 1 supertanker: completed a blockade-line crossing with AIS broadcasting openly along the Oman coast
- Up to 20 empty supertankers: currently available, possibly for loading Iranian crude or for floating storage
- 4 of those 20: outside US sanctions, giving Tehran a narrow legal margin for ownership transfers at sea
- 1 VLCC carrying ~2 million barrels: still inside Iranian waters, anchored further west than expected

## AIS-on transit interpreted as deterrence test
The deliberate decision to keep AIS active during a sanctioned voyage along the Omani coast contrasts sharply with the ghost-fleet pattern of dark transits and transponder spoofing. Analysts view the open broadcast as a demonstration that Iranian operators no longer fear meaningful US enforcement cost. Reuters previously documented several sanctioned supertankers entering the Arabian Gulf despite Washington's warnings, and TankerTrackers data corroborates that pattern.

## Floating storage build-up
Up to 20 empty supertankers position Tehran to absorb surplus crude resulting from supply chain disruptions or to stage future loadings once windows of opportunity open. Four unsanctioned hulls within that pool give Iran flexibility for ownership shuffles, bareboat charters, or ship-to-ship transfers without immediate OFAC exposure.

## Tactical positioning
TankerTrackers also notes that a supertanker holding roughly two million barrels of crude has not yet exited Iranian waters and now sits at a more western anchorage. The shift suggests an evolving Iranian fleet posture under mounting international pressure, with operators apparently waiting for a political breakthrough or a breach in the naval blockade.

## Significance
The combined behavior — AIS-on blockade run, empty supertanker build-up, unsanctioned tonnage, and tactical anchoring — points to a coherent Iranian campaign of resistance tactics that probes US enforcement boundaries while preserving optionality. Each successful crossing erodes deterrence credibility and emboldens shadow-fleet operators across the region.

title: Hormuz transit climbs to 20 crossings as Gulf dark activity surges to 170 events
summary: Windward's April 30 maritime intelligence daily reports that Strait of Hormuz transit rose to 20 crossings on April 29, with broader corridor usage and a return to the Southern Corridor. Gulf vessel presence climbed to 902 while dark activity surged to 170 events, the highest level in recent days. Kharg Island shows mixed loading, and GPS jamming spread near Khor Fakkan.
contents:
# Hormuz Transit Rises to 20 Crossings as Dark Activity Surges in Gulf

## Operational Overview

Maritime activity across the Strait of Hormuz climbed on April 29, with higher transit volumes and broader corridor usage, while deceptive shipping practices expanded across the wider Gulf. Transit flows reached the highest level in recent days, with a notable return to the Southern Corridor alongside sustained outbound movement.

## Transit Across Both Corridors

Transit through the Strait of Hormuz climbed to 20 crossings on April 29, including 6 inbound and 14 outbound movements. All outbound vessels transmitted AIS, while inbound activity included two dark transits. Traffic split across both corridors, with 13 vessels routing through the Northern Corridor and 7 through the Southern Corridor, marking the highest Southern Corridor usage observed in recent days.

## Gulf Activity and Deception

Total vessel presence across the Gulf rose to 902 vessels, indicating a continued rebuild in overall activity. Panama remained the dominant flag state with 137 vessels, followed by Iran (103), Comoros (91), the UAE (75), Marshall Islands (71), and Liberia (67). Dark activity events surged to 170 — the highest level in recent days. This parallel rise in vessel count and dark behavior shows that higher movement coincides with greater reliance on concealment rather than a shift toward transparency.

## Kharg, Bandar Abbas, and IUU Pressure

Electro-Optical imagery identified three dark tankers drifting at Kharg anchorage, while two tankers loaded at the T-jetty terminal. GPS jamming incidents expanded outside the Strait of Hormuz, particularly near Khor Fakkan. Over the past 12 months, 43 Chinese fishing vessels conducted 3,611 fishing operations and 338 dark activity events within Oman's exclusive economic zone, with 12 vessels assessed as high risk for illegal, unreported, and unregulated fishing.

title: Chabahar dark tanker cluster expands to 5 vessels as Iran spoofs AIS off Basrah
summary: Windward's April 29 daily reports that on April 28, Hormuz transit held at 13 crossings under full AIS visibility while dark activity rose to 148 events. A dark tanker cluster near Chabahar expanded to five vessels totaling 7-8 million barrels of capacity, including the Iranian-flagged VLCC DIONA. Ten Iran-trading sanctioned tankers spoof AIS positions off Basrah, Iraq.
contents:
# Chabahar Dark Cluster Grows as Iran-Linked Tankers Spoof AIS off Basrah

## Operational Overview

Maritime activity across the Strait of Hormuz remained stable in volume but increasingly uneven in behavior. On April 28, transit volumes held steady, with all crossings conducted while transmitting AIS. At the same time, Gulf-wide vessel presence declined slightly, while dark activity climbed sharply, indicating renewed reliance on deceptive shipping practices despite visible transit compliance.

## Transit Holds with Full Visibility

Thirteen vessels crossed the Strait, including 4 inbound and 9 outbound transits. All movements transmitted AIS, with no dark transits recorded. A Japanese-owned, Panama-flagged VLCC transited outbound without Iranian linkage, reinforcing that non-sanctioned commercial traffic continues to move under current conditions even as Iran-linked activity remains constrained.

## Chabahar Cluster Expansion

Dark tanker positioning east of Hormuz continues to consolidate near Chabahar. Windward Multi-Source Intelligence identified five dark tankers in the Chabahar port waiting area on April 28. One vessel matched with high confidence the Iranian-flagged VLCC DIONA (IMO 9569695), assessed as having arrived from the Singapore/Malaysia ship-to-ship transfer hub. Two AIS-transmitting Iranian-flagged vessels, the container ship KASHAN and bulk carrier ARTMAN, also appeared in proximity. The five dark tankers represent an estimated 7–8 million barrels of carrying capacity.

## AIS Spoofing off Basrah

Deceptive shipping practices escalated, with 10 Iran-trading, U.S.-sanctioned tankers actively spoofing locations to appear anchored off Basrah, Iraq. Vessels include the VLCCs ALICIA (IMO 9281695) and RHN (IMO 9208215) under fraudulent Curacao registries, STAR FOREST flagged to Hong Kong, and AQUA under a fraudulent Malawi registry. The pattern reflects a long-established evasion tactic where tankers manipulate AIS signals to disguise diversions to Iranian ports for sanctioned crude loading.

title: Iranian crude flows to Asia drop sharply, no VLCC Strait of Malacca crossings since April 24
summary: Windward's April 28 daily reports the U.S. blockade has more than halved Iranian crude shipments reaching Asia over the past 10 days, with no VLCC laden with Iranian crude tracked through the Strait of Malacca since April 24. Hormuz transit held at 13 crossings on April 27 with dark activity falling to 111 events. Approximately 153 million barrels of Iranian oil remain on water. Piracy risk off Somalia escalated, with multiple hijackings.
contents:
# Iranian Crude Flows to Asia Collapse Under U.S. Blockade Pressure

## Operational Overview

Maritime activity across the Strait of Hormuz and surrounding corridors remains constrained but operational, with moderate transit levels and continued reductions in both vessel count and dark activity. On April 27, transit volumes held at a moderate level, while Gulf-wide vessel presence declined and dark activity continued to decrease.

## Iranian Crude Flows Drop Under Blockade

The U.S. blockade has more than halved Iranian crude shipments reaching Asia over the past 10 days, with further declines expected into May. No VLCC laden with Iranian crude has transited the Strait of Malacca since April 24, marking the longest gap since the war began. Windward intelligence shows Iranian crude exports reaching Asia via the Strait averaged 1.3 million barrels per day during the first 28 days of April, well below the 1.9 million barrels per day loaded from Kharg Island in March, and far below the 2.2 million barrels per day Vortexa recorded in February before the war.

## On-Water Storage and Riau STS Hub

Approximately 153 million barrels of Iranian oil remain on the water, much of it held in floating storage west of Hormuz or on vessels that sailed to the Riau Archipelago in Malaysia's exclusive economic zone. The Riau area has long served as a transfer zone for Iranian crude, enabling onward movement to China through ship-to-ship transfers under AIS silence.

## Somalia Piracy Escalation

Piracy risk off Somalia received an upgrade to substantial following a series of hijackings. On April 26, a cargo vessel got redirected by unauthorized individuals near Garacad. The Palau-flagged tanker Honour 25 (IMO 1099735) also got seized days earlier. The Joint Maritime Centre confirmed a Pirate Action Group active in the Somali basin.

contents:
# US Enforcement Extends to Arabian Sea with LPG SEVAN Interception

## Operational Overview

Maritime activity across the Strait of Hormuz and adjacent corridors shows early signs of recovery under sustained enforcement pressure. Transit volumes rebounded on April 25, with all crossings conducted under full AIS visibility, while U.S. enforcement expanded beyond the Gulf with the interception of a sanctioned LPG tanker in the Arabian Sea.

## LPG SEVAN Interdiction

On April 25, U.S. naval forces intercepted the sanctioned LPG tanker LPG SEVAN (IMO 9177806) in the central Arabian Sea, extending enforcement activity well beyond the Strait of Hormuz. The Panama-flagged vessel, assessed as part of the Iranian dark fleet, received OFAC designation on April 24 — one day prior to interception. U.S. Treasury reporting indicates LPG SEVAN previously transported approximately 750,000 barrels of Iranian LPG cargo to Bangladesh between August and November 2025. At the time of interception, the vessel carried approximately 200,600 barrels of LPG plus olefins. While declaring Dubai as its destination, its routing trajectory pointed toward Jaigarh, India. The tanker executed a tight 180-degree turn at approximately 11:08 UTC on April 25, around 200 nautical miles south of Pakistan, consistent with a compelled maneuver following interception by a helicopter deployed from USS Pinckney.

## Kharg Island Loading and Queue

SAR imagery from April 26 at 02:37 UTC identified two tankers actively loading at Kharg Island terminals — one VLCC and one Suezmax-class vessel at the eastern and western berths, with combined loading capacity estimated at approximately 3 million barrels. At least eight additional VLCCs anchored south and east of Kharg, forming a substantial waiting queue and indicating elevated pressure on export throughput.

## Transit Rebound

Nineteen vessels crossed the Strait on April 25, including five inbound and fourteen outbound transits. All crossings conducted with AIS active, marking a complete absence of dark transits. Total vessel presence across the Gulf rose to 892 vessels, an increase of 126 compared to the previous day.

contents:
# IRGC Attacks Three Outbound Containers in Hormuz, Seizes MSC FRANCESCA

## Operational Overview

Maritime dynamics across the Strait of Hormuz continue to shift from constrained activity toward selective escalation. Operational risk escalated on April 22, when IRGC forces attacked three outbound container vessels and seized at least one, marking an escalation to direct vessel interdiction.

## Vessel Attacks

Three outbound container vessels came under coordinated attack within a narrow operational window, all while transiting with AIS switched off and resuming transmission in or near the attack zone. MSC FRANCESCA (IMO 9401116), a Panama-flagged container vessel, came under fire at approximately 04:55 UTC around 15 nautical miles northeast of Oman while transiting outbound from Saudi Arabia. An IRGC gunboat engaged the vessel without prior VHF challenge, causing damage to the hull and accommodation. The vessel got seized and escorted into Iranian territorial waters. EPAMINONDAS (IMO 9153862), a Liberia-flagged container vessel departing the UAE, came under fire shortly after at 05:00 UTC with small arms and RPGs, sustaining minor bridge damage. EUPHORIA (IMO 9235828), a Panama-flagged container vessel, also came under fire while transiting outbound toward Jeddah. The crew successfully cleared the Strait into the Gulf of Oman.

## Kharg Island Exports Drop

Kharg Island recorded its lowest weekly crude export volume since the war began. Between April 13 and 19, total departures came to approximately 3 million barrels, significantly below the typical weekly average of around 8 million barrels. EO imagery from April 18 shows no active loading at the terminals, a rare pause not observed since the start of the conflict. Loading partially resumed on April 20.

## Chabahar Cluster

A cluster of 8 large tankers formed near Chabahar, representing approximately 14 million barrels of capacity. These patterns point to adaptation under pressure rather than a breakdown in flows.

title: Two weeks into Hormuz ceasefire: fragmentation, attacks, and U.S. enforcement expansion
summary: Windward's two-week ceasefire breakdown shows the Strait of Hormuz remains active only in a restricted and highly unstable sense. The brief signal of reopening got overtaken by renewed closure messaging, vessel attacks, and large-scale course reversals. contents:
# Two Weeks Into the Ceasefire: A Fragmented Maritime System

## Hormuz Remains Active but No Longer Stable

Two weeks after the ceasefire announcement, the maritime system has moved further away from normalization. Transit through Hormuz remains possible, but no longer under anything resembling a stable access regime. On April 17, despite public Iranian statements, the mid-section of the Strait remained empty as operators held position rather than committing to transit. On April 18, traffic surged temporarily, with 35 vessels transiting before renewed closure messaging triggered 13 reversals in the immediate aftermath. Transit collapsed to just three vessels on April 19, the lowest level since the blockade began.

## U.S. Enforcement Expanded Beyond the Strait

The second week marked a clear geographic and operational expansion of U.S. enforcement. U.S. Central Command confirmed multiple Iran-linked oil tankers got intercepted and instructed by radio to reverse course. By April 19, the directive expanded to authorize boarding, search, and seizure of Iran-linked and sanctioned vessels on the high seas worldwide. The first confirmed U.S. interdiction of an Iranian-linked vessel outside the immediate Strait zone occurred on April 20, when the OFAC-sanctioned Iran-flagged containership TOUSKA got intercepted in the Gulf of Oman while approaching Chabahar from Malaysia.

## Iranian Exports Adapt

Iranian export flows continued throughout the second week, but Kharg Island showed more visible pressure. Average daily export volumes from Kharg between February and April held at roughly 2.04 million barrels per day, with two confirmed VLCC departures around the start of the blockade, both bound for Dongjiakou, China. By April 23, total crude departures from the island over the prior week came to approximately 3 million barrels, far below the typical weekly average.

## Bandar Abbas and Chabahar Adaptation

Bandar Abbas remained a clear indicator of non-transparent maritime activity. By April 21, Windward detected 296 vessels in the area, with only 74 transmitting AIS. A growing cluster of large tankers near Chabahar suggests enforcement pressure forces eastward repositioning rather than restoring flow through the Gulf.

title: Iran war exposes AIS gaps: 1,650+ vessels affected, multi-source intelligence shifts model
summary: Windward analysis shows the Iran war exposed fundamental limits in AIS-based maritime visibility, with more than 1,650 vessels experiencing GPS and AIS interference. Dark activity, AIS spoofing, and controlled routing made vessel tracking unreliable. Two non-AIS-transmitting VLCCs loaded approximately 4 million barrels at Kharg Island, only detected via satellite imagery. Multi-source intelligence enables verification beyond self-reported signals.
contents:
# Iran War Exposes AIS Gaps and the Need for Multi-Source Intelligence

## The Iran War Exposed a Visibility Gap

Operation Epic Fury did not stop maritime activity. It changed how that activity gets conducted and how visible it remains. From the outset, the electronic environment degraded at scale. More than 1,100 vessels experienced GPS and AIS interference within the first 24 hours, with signals displaced onto airports, inland infrastructure, and artificial clusters across the Gulf. As the conflict progressed, that disruption expanded to more than 1,650 vessels, with persistent jamming zones, signal distortion, and false positioning becoming a sustained feature of the operating environment.

## AIS Shows Intent, Not Reality

AIS remains the backbone of maritime awareness for most organizations, but it functions fundamentally as a declarative system. It tells operators what a vessel reports, where it claims to position itself, and where it says it heads. It does not tell operators whether that information remains accurate, whether the vessel operates without transmission, or whether its behavior matches its declaration. During the Iran war, vessels disabled AIS during loading operations, spoofed identities and destinations, operated along controlled corridors under military oversight, and executed irregular routing patterns including U-turns and holding behavior.

## Kharg Island Loading Detected Without AIS

At Kharg Island, two non-AIS-transmitting VLCCs got detected loading approximately 4 million barrels via satellite imagery, while not broadcasting positions. This demonstrates that critical activity continues even when AIS shows no presence.

## Multi-Source Intelligence Changes the Model

Multi-source intelligence addresses the core gap exposed in this conflict: the difference between what gets reported and what actually happens at sea. Synthetic Aperture Radar provides all-weather, day/night vessel detection independent of AIS. Electro-Optical imagery delivers visual confirmation of vessel identity and activity. Radio Frequency signal analysis enables detection and geolocation of radio frequency emissions. Behavioral analytics connects signals into actionable insight, highlighting suspicious or non-compliant activity.

summary: Windward's Q1 2026 Risk Report documents the most turbulent shipping quarter in 50 years. The Iran war effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz, dropping traffic by 97% and stranding over 800 vessels. Vessel interdictions surged 160% with 13 ships boarded and detained. The shadow fleet expanded to 2,108 vessels, with 65.5% sanctioned. GPS jamming reached 978,000 incidents, impacting more than 1,100 vessels.
contents:
# Q1 2026 Risk Report: Shipping's Most Turbulent Quarter in 50 Years

## A Quarter Defined by Geopolitical Shock

On February 28, the Iran war effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz. Within days, daily traffic through the world's most critical oil chokepoint collapsed from roughly 120 vessels to a trickle — a 97% drop that left more than 800 ships stranded west of the strait, thousands of seafarers in limbo, and Asian refiners scrambling for crude that could no longer reach them. The quarter opened with the capture of Venezuelan president Nicolás Maduro, the culmination of a six-week U.S. naval blockade designed to choke off sanctioned oil exports.

## Enforcement Escalates: Interdictions and Legal Precedents

The blockade drove a global record in vessel interdictions targeting stateless and falsely flagged shadow fleet tankers. Stateless tankers that evaded the Venezuelan blockade got tracked and detained across the Atlantic and Indian Oceans in January. The UK, France, Sweden, and Belgium all detained tankers during the quarter. Thirteen ships got boarded and detained, up 160% from the prior quarter, accounting for half of all interdictions seen across 2025 and 2026. Of these, 92% had sanctions designations and 85% flew false flags.

## The Rise of Fraudulent Registries

Some 290 internationally trading tankers broadcast fraudulent registry flags as Q1 closed. Windward identified 20 fraudulent registries, as defined by the IMO. 88% of vessels using fraudulent registries faced Western sanctions. The most frequently used fraudulent registry: Netherlands Antilles (52 vessels), followed by Guyana (43), Guinea (28), and Madagascar (24).

## Dark Fleet and GPS Jamming

The shadow fleet expanded by 45 vessels to 2,108, with 65.5% sanctioned. GPS jamming surged sharply, with 978,000 incidents recorded in Q1. 98% occurred in the Middle East, impacting more than 1,100 vessels.

## Oil Flows Collapse

Global crude loadings fell 7.5% quarter-over-quarter to 41.1 million barrels per day, driven by a 76% month-over-month drop in March exports west of Hormuz. Of the 142.5 million barrels loaded from the region in March, 128 million remained in transit or floating storage due to the closure.

contents:
# Brief Hormuz Reopening Reversed: 35 Vessels Turn Around as Attacks Resume

## Operational Overview

Maritime conditions in and around the Strait of Hormuz deteriorated sharply, with the brief signal of reopening overtaken by renewed closure, vessel attacks, and large-scale course reversals. Iran's public messaging remained contradictory: on April 17, Iranian officials declared the Strait open to commercial shipping during the ceasefire, while also insisting that transit would remain conditional, coordinated, and subject to reversal if the U.S. blockade continued. By April 18, IRGC media announced that the Strait had closed again, and vessel behavior immediately reflected that shift.

## 35 Vessels Reverse Course

Windward tracked 35 outbound vessels reversing course over 36 hours, while 13 reversals occurred in the immediate aftermath of the April 18 closure announcement alone. This reversal pattern extended beyond Iran-linked shipping. A total of 33 vessels with no direct Iran touchpoint also reversed course on April 18, including two LNG carriers and nine crude tankers — among them five VLCCs, three Suezmaxes, and one Aframax.

## Vessel Attacks

Three additional vessel incidents occurred on April 18, bringing the total number of vessels attacked to 29. SANMAR HERALD, an India-flagged VLCC+ carrying approximately 1.848 million barrels of crude, came under fire from two IRGC gunboats at 09:20 UTC, approximately 20 nautical miles northeast of Oman. The vessel reportedly held prior Iranian transit clearance, but came under fire without warning. A container ship also got struck by an unknown projectile at approximately 11:25 UTC, around 25 nautical miles northeast of Oman. JAG ARNAV, an India-flagged bulk carrier, reported a projectile splash at 12:08 UTC around three nautical miles off the Omani coast.

## Iranian Cargo Network Globally Active

Iranian cargo flows remain globally extensive and highly deceptive. More than 177 tankers carrying Iranian cargo currently move on the water worldwide, most of them concentrated toward Asian and Middle Eastern destinations. Across that fleet, 163 sail under fraudulent flag registries with elevated Iranian sanctions compliance risk, while at least 719 Iranian dark fleet tankers receive global tracking.

summary: Windward's April 15 daily covers day two of the U.S. blockade with outbound-heavy transit through the Strait of Hormuz. Vessel behavior reflected fragmentation, including U-turns, drifting, dark activity, and continued selective movement. Sanctioned and falsely flagged vessels including CHRISTIANNA, ELPIS, and RICH STARRY tested enforcement boundaries. Kharg Island showed continued loading and a pre-blockade VLCC departure.
contents:
# US Blockade Day Two: Fragmented Vessel Response, Iranian Loading Continues

## Operational Overview

The U.S. maritime blockade of Iranian ports started shaping vessel behavior in real time, but it has not yet produced a uniform halt in movement through and around the Strait of Hormuz. At sea, vessel-level behavior reflects a fragmented picture. Some vessels reverse course, others drift after clearing the Strait, while some continue operating under reduced visibility or inconsistent routing patterns.

## Outbound Movement Surges

Transit through Hormuz remained active on April 14, but the balance of movement shifted sharply toward outbound traffic. A total of 19 vessels transited the Strait, including 5 inbound and 14 outbound crossings. Inbound traffic consisted of two tankers flagged to Madagascar and Panama, alongside three cargo vessels. Outbound traffic included two tankers flagged to Malawi and the Netherlands, one Panama-flagged bulk carrier, and eleven cargo vessels.

## Vessel-Level Responses

CHRISTIANNA (IMO 9596703), a Liberia-flagged Panamax bulk carrier, sailed from Bandar Imam Khomeini on April 10 and transited outbound through Hormuz around 20:00 BST on April 13. After clearing the Strait, the vessel went dark for approximately 15 hours, later reappearing in Omani waters before executing a U-turn. ELPIS (IMO 9212400), a falsely flagged and U.S.-sanctioned tanker, transited outbound through the Strait on April 13, then went dark in the Gulf of Oman. RICH STARRY (IMO 9773301), a falsely flagged, U.S.-sanctioned handysize tanker carrying Iranian cargo, abandoned an outbound transit on April 13 approximately one hour before the blockade came into effect, then resumed transit eight hours later, and ultimately turned around again on April 14.

## Kharg Island Loading and Pre-Blockade Departure

A SAR image from April 12 at 17:42 UTC shows three VLCCs loading at Kharg Island. By April 13 at 05:40 UTC, only two VLCCs remained, indicating that one vessel likely departed during this window, just hours before the blockade took effect. The departed vessel likely carried Iranian crude bound for China.

title: Hormuz after ceasefire: controlled access via Larak corridor, 3,200 vessels stranded
summary: Windward analysis finds the ceasefire has not reopened the Strait of Hormuz. Transit remains tightly controlled through IRGC-managed routing rather than standard commercial lanes. Iran signals a shift toward formalized control mechanisms including potential tolls of approximately $1 per barrel. Approximately 3,200 vessels remain stranded west of Hormuz, including ~800 tankers and cargo ships. Europe-Gulf voyages now take 41 days instead of 25, with ~25% higher costs.
contents:
# Hormuz After the Ceasefire: A Controlled System, Not a Recovery

## A Ceasefire Without Reopening

A ceasefire has gone into effect, but the maritime system has not reset. Transit through the Strait of Hormuz remains restricted, coordinated, and selectively enforced. There has not yet emerged any return to open commercial navigation. Standard shipping lanes remain largely unused, and no meaningful increase in traffic has followed the ceasefire announcement. This reflects a supervised pause, where operational control remains intact, and geopolitical leverage continues active exercise.

## Transit Remains Selective and Constrained

On April 8, five bulk carriers transited outbound through the Strait, all moving through the IRGC-controlled corridor around Larak Island rather than through standard commercial shipping lanes. By April 9, only limited additional movements occurred, including one inbound handysize bulk carrier, a small outbound product tanker, and a sanctioned, falsely flagged LPG carrier carrying Iranian LPG outbound after previously aborting its transit attempt.

## Control Moves Toward Formalization

Iran signals its intent to institutionalize its role in governing transit through Hormuz. Proposed measures include mandatory pre-transit coordination and approval, vessel inspection requirements, defined transit corridors, and potential toll systems based on cargo type. Public messaging indicates a potential charge of approximately $1 per barrel for outbound oil shipments, with inbound cargo exempt.

## The Cost of Avoiding Hormuz

The disruption has now embedded directly into global shipping economics. A standard Europe–Gulf container rotation that would typically take 25 days via Suez and Hormuz now requires approximately 41 days via the Cape of Good Hope. This shift adds roughly 6,500 nautical miles and 15 sailing days. Operating costs increase by an estimated $300–400 per TEU, compounded by emergency bunker surcharges and war-risk premiums. Freight rates on Europe-Gulf lanes now run approximately 25% above pre-crisis levels.

## Stranded Vessels and Diversion Hubs

Approximately 3,200 vessels, including nearly 800 tankers and cargo ships, remain positioned west of Hormuz awaiting clarity. Container flows have reorganized into a five-node diversion system centered on Salalah, Sohar, Khor Fakkan, Fujairah, and Jebel Ali.

contents:
# TankerTrackers Identifies Five Tankers Loading Iranian Oil Despite US Blockade

## Headline Findings
TankerTrackers, an independent oil shipment tracker, identified five empty Iranian tankers loading millions of barrels of oil at Iranian Persian Gulf ports as the US naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz continues. The Washington Post relied on TankerTrackers satellite analysis to confirm the loading activity. Three tankers appeared at Kharg Island, where roughly 90% of Iran's oil gets processed and shipped, while two more loaded at Bandar Mahshahr, Iran's northernmost Persian Gulf port.

## Cargo Volumes
- 5 million barrels: combined carrying capacity of the three tankers spotted at Kharg Island
- Approximately 9 million barrels: estimated cargo on five tankers spotted earlier in the Gulf of Oman, no longer visible in current imagery

## Floating Storage Strategy
TankerTrackers analysis suggests Iran's reason for storing oil at Kharg and other terminals does not involve immediate export. Instead, the tankers serve as floating oil storage to keep terminals functioning. Kharg Island terminals require constant flow of oil to avoid damage, with the tankers acting as buffer capacity that prevents shut-ins. Etras Katinas of the Royal United Services Institute told The Washington Post that this approach allows Iran to keep oil moving out of the upstream system to avoid a buildup that would force production shut-ins.

## Blockade Context
US officials reported that no Iranian vessels have broken the blockade since its imposition on April 13. CENTCOM stated that 21 ships complied and returned to Iran after warnings from the guided-missile destroyer USS Michael Murphy patrolling the Arabian Sea. The location of the five tankers near Iran's Chabahar Port, close to the blockade line, could not get determined from analyzed imagery.

title: TankerTrackers cites Sentinel-1 imagery: 13 vessels anchored east of Kharg Island double pre-blockade levels
summary: Bloomberg, citing TankerTrackers analysis of European Union Sentinel-1 satellite imagery from April 20, reports a VLCC capable of hauling 2 million barrels moored at Kharg Island's jetty alongside 13 vessels anchored east of the island — roughly double the count from the day before the US blockade began on April 13. The buildup suggests Iran accelerated loading operations since blockade enforcement began.
contents:
# Sentinel-1 Satellite Imagery Confirms Kharg Island Loading Surge — TankerTrackers

## Overview
TankerTrackers analysis cited by Bloomberg and Times of India shows a clear surge in tanker activity at Iran's Kharg Island export hub despite the US Strait of Hormuz blockade. European Union Sentinel-1 satellite imagery captured on Monday April 20, 2026 reveals a very large crude carrier moored at the Kharg Island jetty — capable of hauling around 2 million barrels of oil. A comparable image from Saturday April 18 showed no ships docked at the same berth.

## Anchorage Buildup
- 13 vessels: anchored east of Kharg Island on April 20, most of them very large crude carriers
- Roughly half that count: anchored east of the island on April 12, the day before the blockade began
- 2 million barrels: estimated capacity of the single VLCC at the jetty

The buildup of waiting tankers indicates that Iran accelerated loading operations following the start of the blockade. With no evidence yet that significant volumes successfully cleared US enforcement, analysts told Bloomberg that the crude loaded onto these tankers likely fills floating storage rather than reaching foreign buyers.

## Storage Runway
Iran has roughly 90 million barrels of available crude storage. Even if the US blockade entirely shuts exports, FGE NexantECA analysts cited by Bloomberg estimate Iran could maintain its current production rate of around 3.5 million barrels per day for another two months before Tehran would face forced output cuts. Loading additional tankers gives Iran more time. Miad Maleki, a senior fellow at the Foundation for Defense of Democracies and former US Treasury sanctions official, told Bloomberg that the loading activity provides relief from running out of storage. JPMorgan analyst Natasha Kaneva and her team wrote in an April 21 note that if the blockade holds, it would constrain volumes mechanically rather than just financially, leaving far less room for workaround trade.

## Enforcement Reach
Beyond the Gulf of Oman, US forces boarded the tanker Majestic X carrying Iranian oil in the Indian Ocean, days after intercepting the Tifani roughly halfway between Sri Lanka and the Strait of Malacca. Both ships face US sanctions. The seizures signal Washington's willingness to enforce the blockade well beyond waters immediately surrounding Iran.

contents:
## TankerTrackers data drives Reuters update on Iran blockade exit flows

### Headline numbers cited from TankerTrackers.com

TankerTrackers.com satellite analysis, quoted by Reuters correspondent Jonathan Saul, documents that **six tankers carrying roughly 10.5 million barrels of Iranian crude** turned back to Iranian ports during the past several days under U.S. Navy pressure. Two additional tankers carrying about **4 million barrels** slipped past the blockade on April 24 bound for Asia. Four empty Iranian tankers reappeared near Pakistan's coast after returning from Asian destinations, suggesting attempts to reposition tonnage outside the enforcement zone.

### Strait of Hormuz traffic collapse

Kpler tracking and SynMax satellite data complement the TankerTrackers reporting:

- Pre-war traffic: 125–140 ships per day crossing the Strait
- April 26 traffic: just **seven crossings**, none carrying export crude
- Iranian-flagged dry bulker *Bavand* departed from Iranian port
- Other transits originated from Iraqi ports, not Iran

### Enforcement totals and context

U.S. Central Command stated 37 vessels turned back since the blockade began on April 13. Ship broker Clarksons noted Iran continues detaining ships that fail to meet Tehran's transit requirements while U.S. forces continue active enforcement. Analysts told Reuters that U.S. forces have diverted Iran-linked ships as far east as the Malacca Strait, leaving cargo destinations uncertain.

### Humanitarian dimension

IMO Secretary-General Arsenio Dominguez warned a committee session that hundreds of ships and roughly **20,000 seafarers** remain stranded inside the Persian Gulf. He flagged psychological strain on crews and elevated risk of accidents, including environmental incidents, the longer the standoff persists.

title: Russian shadow fleet idles in Gulf of Finland as Ukrainian strikes paralyze Baltic oil terminals
summary: Moscow Times reports 30 to 40 Russian shadow-fleet tankers cluster in Russian and international waters of the Gulf of Finland, awaiting damaged terminals at Primorsk and Ust-Luga to come back online after Ukrainian air strikes. contents:
## Baltic shadow-fleet cluster grows as Russian export terminals lie damaged

### Vessel cluster characteristics

The Moscow Times documented dozens of Russian state-linked shadow-fleet tankers loitering off the Gulf of Finland, with estimates running 30 to 40 vessels. Most anchor inside Russian waters or within the six-nautical-mile international strip separating Finnish and Estonian waters. Their stated reason for delay: Ukrainian air strikes have knocked oil-loading terminals at **Primorsk** and **Ust-Luga** offline, with vessels showing ETA windows ranging from April 16 to May 18.

### Security and environmental concerns

Elisabeth Braw, senior fellow at the Atlantic Council, told the publication these vessels carry direct ties to the Russian state. Russia's open acknowledgement of those ties heightens the chance Moscow could weaponize the cluster for adversarial activity such as sabotage of subsea cables or sensors. Braw added that international maritime law grants every ship the right of innocent passage, which limits NATO Operation Baltic Sentry options for direct interdiction:

Even though the shadow-fleet vessels draw little welcome from Helsinki or Tallinn, Braw stressed they retain the right to wait offshore until Russian ports return to service.

Ksenia Vakhrusheva of the Bellona Foundation flagged additional spill risks tied to aging hulls operating outside mainstream insurance coverage.

### Wider shadow-fleet context

Researchers tracked by The Moscow Times put the global Russian shadow fleet at more than **3,000 vessels**, many sailing under flags of convenience with concealed ownership chains and crews potentially linked to private military companies. Russian seaborne crude exports surged 115 percent month-on-month in March, with nearly half routed through shadow tonnage as Strait of Hormuz disruptions reroute global flows. Urals crude touched **$116 per barrel** during the spike.

title: IRGC fires on tanker and strikes container ship northeast of Oman as Hormuz blockade tightens
summary: UKMTO logged two Iranian attacks on civilian shipping near the Strait of Hormuz on April 18. Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps boats opened fire on a tanker 20 nautical miles northeast of Oman at 09:20 UTC, then a container ship took an unidentified projectile hit 25 nautical miles northeast of Oman at 11:25 UTC, damaging containers. Tehran reimposed a transit ban after Washington tightened blockade rules.
contents:
## Iran reimposes Hormuz blockade with two coordinated strikes on commercial shipping

### UKMTO incident timeline

The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations centre logged two attacks within hours along the Strait of Hormuz approach. Militarnyi reported the sequence:

- **09:20 UTC, 20nm NE of Oman**: Two IRGC fast boats approached a tanker without radio contact and opened fire. The tanker and crew sustained no reported injuries.
- **11:25 UTC, 25nm NE of Oman**: A container ship reported a projectile strike that damaged on-deck containers. Crew avoided injury and the vessel reported no fire onboard.

### Strategic backdrop

Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi declared on April 8 that the Strait of Hormuz remained "fully open" to commercial vessels following the Lebanon ceasefire that took hold April 16, although Tehran restricted passage to corridors approved by Iran's Ports and Maritime Organization. Iran cited residual sea-mine fields it could not fully clear.

Donald Trump ordered the U.S. Navy to begin a complete Hormuz blockade on April 12 and to detain any vessel that paid Iranian transit fees. Washington widened the blockade on April 16 to encompass all Iranian and sanctioned vessels worldwide. By April 18, civilian crews received radio messages from the Iranian Navy declaring the strait closed in retaliation against the U.S. enforcement effort.

### Implications for shipping

The attacks confirmed a return to direct kinetic harassment of merchant traffic and forced traders, insurers, and military planners to treat the Hormuz transit as a high-threat corridor. TankerTrackers and other intelligence providers continue to track which vessels accept the risk, which divert via the Cape route, and which loiter in standby anchorages off Oman and the UAE.

title: Windward week three update: Hormuz transits hit 20 on April 29 as Bandar Abbas hosts 70-vessel dark cluster

Strait of Hormuz transits climbed to 20 crossings on April 29, the highest of the week, while Gulf-wide vessel presence reached 902 with 170 dark events. Iran continued routing tonnage through Bandar Abbas and the Larak Island chokepoint, and the LPG tanker SEVAN remained interdicted in the Arabian Sea.
contents:
## Three weeks after Hormuz ceasefire, traffic recovers unevenly under enforcement pressure

### Strait of Hormuz transit ramp

Windward documented 20 crossings on April 29, six inbound and 14 outbound, the highest weekly tally and a clear signal that operators continue to test enforcement risk. Daily transit progression across the week:

- April 25: 19 crossings (5 in / 14 out)
- April 26: 8 crossings (split)
- April 27: 13 crossings (3 in / 10 out)
- April 28: 13 crossings (4 in / 9 out)
- April 29: 20 crossings (6 in / 14 out)

Northern Corridor handled the bulk of outbound tanker traffic on April 29, while Southern Corridor served cargo movements toward India and East Africa.

### Gulf vessel presence and dark activity

Gulf-wide vessel counts climbed across the week:

- April 25: 892 vessels, 123 dark events
- April 26: 920 vessels, 117 dark events
- April 27: 896 vessels, 111 dark events
- April 28: 890 vessels, 148 dark events
- April 29: 902 vessels, 170 dark events

Dark events surged 53 percent from April 27 to April 29 even as overall vessel counts stayed nearly flat, signaling that operators turned to AIS spoofing and switch-offs to mask movements rather than absent themselves.

### Bandar Abbas and Larak Island chokepoint

Windward analysts highlighted the Bandar Abbas approach as the densest dark-vessel cluster, with roughly 70 vessels loitering and showing intermittent AIS. The Larak Island chokepoint hosted a narrow corridor of dark traffic hugging the Iranian coastline. Chabahar continued to host 5 to 7 dark tankers acting as a staging zone for ship-to-ship transfers.

### Enforcement and threat highlights

U.S. forces interdicted the LPG tanker **SEVAN** in the Arabian Sea, extending sanctioned-vessel enforcement beyond the Gulf perimeter. Windward also flagged AIS spoofing off Basrah, IUU fishing inside Oman's EEZ, and GPS jamming around Khor Fakkan, all of which complicate vessel tracking.

title: Windward analysis: Iran war turns chokepoints into priced assets, Hormuz exports collapse from 30M to 8.4M bpd
summary: Maya Romi's April 23 Windward essay argues the Iran conflict transforms maritime chokepoints from neutral conduits into politically controlled, monetizable assets. Hormuz export volume fell from over 30 million barrels per day pre-conflict to 8.4 million on March 6. Cape of Good Hope traffic surged to 383 transits in five days, and a single Europe-Gulf reroute added 6,500 nautical miles, 15 sailing days, and an estimated $300-400 per TEU.
contents:
## Iran war recasts maritime chokepoints as instruments of state power

### Hormuz no longer functions as a neutral artery

Windward analyst Maya Romi argues that the Strait of Hormuz now operates under selective access controlled by political alignment, enforcement posture, and real-time risk tolerance. Iran has floated transit fees on vessels crossing Hormuz, while Indonesia openly discusses tolls along the Strait of Malacca, citing its strategic position. Each move signals a wider shift from free passage to permission-based, priced transit.

### Numerical impact of the conflict on global flows

The essay assembles concrete metrics:

- Daily Hormuz crossings dropped from 70-80 vessels to single digits within the first week
- Multiple days recorded fewer than five transits
- Hormuz export volume crashed from over **30 million barrels per day** before the war to **8.4 million barrels per day on March 6**
- Cape of Good Hope traffic surged to **383 transits between March 7 and March 11**, averaging roughly 77 crossings per day
- A single rerouted Europe-Gulf container voyage added approximately 6,500 nautical miles and 15 sailing days
- Per-TEU costs rose **$300-400** and freight rates pushed roughly 25 percent above pre-crisis baselines

### Other chokepoints under similar logic

Romi extends the analysis to Bab el-Mandeb, the Turkish Straits, and the Panama Canal, arguing each carries comparable potential for political control and pricing. Once states treat chokepoints as monetizable assets, every routing decision becomes a geopolitical decision and cost models must price access risk alongside distance.

### Strategic implications

Shipowners, charterers, and insurers now require dynamic risk pricing that accounts for political access, not just navigational hazards or fuel cost. The essay calls on operators to integrate diplomatic, sanctions, and enforcement signals into routing models, since traditional commercial calculus alone fails to explain present routing patterns.

**Tags**: windward-ai, chokepoints, hormuz, malacca, geopolitics, trade-disruption

title: Windward catalogues 71 confirmed Iran-war maritime incidents: 34 vessels, 37 oil and port assets struck
summary: Maya Romi's April 22 Windward analysis catalogues 71 confirmed maritime incidents during the Iran war: 34 vessel attacks and 37 strikes on oil, gas, and port infrastructure. Tankers and bulk carriers dominate the vessel list. Confirmed strikes span anchored, in-transit, drifting, drydock, and ship-to-ship operations across Hormuz, the Arabian Gulf, Gulf of Oman, Bahrain, and the Northern Arabian Gulf.
contents:
## Windward documents 71 confirmed maritime attacks across Iran war theatres

### Aggregate incident counts

As of April 22, Windward records:

- **71 total confirmed incidents**
- **34 vessel attacks**
- **37 strikes on oil, gas, and port infrastructure**
- Geographies: Strait of Hormuz, Arabian Gulf, Gulf of Oman, Bahrain, Northern Arabian Gulf

Tankers and bulk carriers account for the majority of vessel-side attacks, confirming that core commercial shipping lanes remain the primary exposure zone.

### Multi-vector threat environment

Attack vectors recorded by Windward include:

- Projectile strikes (likely missile or rocket fired)
- UAV-launched munitions
- Uncrewed surface vessel (USV) detonations
- Close-proximity charge blasts during ship-to-ship operations

Operating profiles affected span anchored, in-transit, drifting, drydock, and STS configurations, meaning operators cannot assume safety simply by stopping or holding offshore.

### Selected named incidents

- **SKYLIGHT** (IMO 9330020) — Severe strike at anchor in Strait of Hormuz; projectile hit caused fire; four crew injured during evacuation.
- **HERCULES STAR** (IMO 9916135) — Severe outbound strike in Strait of Hormuz; projectile hit triggered onboard fire.
- **OCEAN ELECTRA** (IMO 9402782) — Direct strike in Arabian Gulf; close-proximity detonation reported with no damage and no injuries.
- **MKD VYOM** (IMO 9284386) — Severe strike from a USV during northbound transit through Gulf of Oman; one fatality confirmed.
- **STENA IMPERATIVE** (IMO 9666077) — Severe strike at Port of Bahrain berth; two projectiles caused one shoreside fatality and two mariner injuries.

### Pattern and exposure assessment

Windward's catalogue confirms that risk extends well beyond Hormuz transit. Attackers reach vessels at anchor, in territorial waters, and at port. Operators servicing the region must treat anchorage, berth, and offshore positions as active threat zones rather than safe-haven alternatives to transit.

**Tags**: windward-ai, iran-war, vessel-attacks, hormuz, infrastructure-strikes

title: Windward April 20 daily: Hormuz drops to 3 transits, 7 VLCCs cluster off Chabahar with ~14M-barrel capacity
summary: Windward's April 20 maritime intelligence daily records the lowest Hormuz transit count of the blockade so far: just three vessels on April 19. The Gulf hosted 870 vessels, dark activity stayed at 140 events, and seven VLCCs gathered near Chabahar with combined potential capacity of roughly 14 million barrels. U.S. enforcement extended into the Gulf of Oman.
contents:
## Hormuz traffic hits blockade-era low as VLCC cluster forms off Chabahar

### Strait transit slumps to three vessels

Windward logged just **three transits** through the Strait of Hormuz on April 19: one inbound and two outbound. Crew and flag breakdown:

- Inbound: Hong Kong-flagged general cargo via the Northern Corridor
- Outbound: Angola-flagged tanker via Northern Corridor; Sri Lanka-flagged cargo via Southern Corridor
- Zero dark transits recorded

Inbound counts dropped seven vessels and outbound counts dropped 25 versus April 18, showing the deterrent effect of the April 18 vessel attacks.

### Gulf-wide presence and dark activity

Gulf vessel presence fell to 870, a daily drop of 49. Dark activity held steady at 140 events. Top flag distribution included:

- Panama: 138
- Iran: 102
- Comoros: 84
- Marshall Islands: 73
- Liberia: 66
- UAE: 62

Fleet composition: 155 bulk carriers, 136 product tankers, 75 crude tankers, 65 container ships, 40 LNG/LPG carriers, 32 chemical tankers.

### Chabahar VLCC cluster

Windward identified seven VLCCs near the port of Chabahar along with one ship-to-ship transfer involving two smaller tankers offshore. Combined capacity of the cluster: approximately **14 million barrels**. One vessel near the southern edge appeared possibly Iranian-flagged, complicating attribution given AIS gaps.

Open-source reports tracked roughly 11 million barrels of Iranian oil routed via the Gulf of Oman since April 13, although AIS confirmed no tanker movements consistent with that volume. The discrepancy underlines reliance on satellite imagery and multi-source intelligence to capture flows hidden from AIS.

### Eastern positioning and evasion implications

The Chabahar cluster suggests Iran continues to reposition tonnage outside the Hormuz enforcement perimeter, potentially using STS transfers and secondary anchorages. U.S. forces concurrently expanded the enforcement footprint into the Gulf of Oman, completing the first interdiction outside the Strait.

**Tags**: windward-ai, hormuz, chabahar, vlcc, dark-fleet, ais-gaps

title: Windward April 27 daily: 8 Hormuz transits with full AIS, ATEELA 1 supports Chabahar dark cluster

Dark activity dropped to 117 events. The sanctioned Iran-flagged products tanker ATEELA 1 (IMO 9548990) now provides bunkering services that sustain a seven-vessel dark cluster off Chabahar.
contents:
## Hormuz transits trickle back as ATEELA 1 powers Chabahar dark cluster

### Strait of Hormuz transit detail

Windward recorded eight crossings on April 26, four inbound and four outbound, all with active AIS. Highlights:

- Inbound: one Panama-flagged product tanker, three cargo vessels (one Comoros, two India)
- Outbound: two bulk carriers (Barbados, St. Kitts and Nevis), two cargo vessels (Panama, Comoros)
- All inbound tanker traffic routed through the Northern Corridor; cargo entered via Southern Corridor
- Zero dark transits for the second consecutive day

### Gulf-wide presence and dark activity

- Panama: 146
- Iran: 100
- Comoros: 89
- Marshall Islands: 76
- Liberia: 71
- UAE: 71

Fleet composition: 156 bulk carriers, 146 product tankers, 83 crude tankers, 62 container ships, 43 LNG/LPG carriers, 38 chemical tankers. Dark activity declined slightly to 117 events, a roughly five percent drop, even as raw vessel presence climbed.

### Chabahar dark cluster gains a bunkering enabler

Windward identified seven tankers operating without AIS off Chabahar — six VLCCs and one Suezmax. The sanctioned Iran-flagged products tanker **ATEELA 1** (IMO 9548990) now broadcasts AIS in the area while quietly providing bunkering services that allow the dark cluster to loiter without departing for fuel. One Iran-flagged sanctioned tanker also operates in the area with AIS active, suggesting Tehran tolerates partial visibility when cargo flows demand it.

### Operational implications

The pattern demonstrates how Iran combines fully visible logistics enablers with concealed crude carriers to maintain export staging outside the Hormuz enforcement zone. Compliance teams should treat ATEELA 1 and similar bunkering vessels as red-flag indicators of nearby dark-fleet activity.
